steps.text: Initiate and develop proposed NPIs within the scope of authority or significantly affecting the operations of programs.

Request review and clearance by appropriate directorates, regional offices, Office of the Solicitor (SOL), the Assistant Secretary, etc., based on the subject and intended application of the proposed NPI.
Provide proposed NPI and necessary background information to ensure the proposed issuance is thoroughly understood and considered.
Establish a due date for response return.
Review and consider comments received and address them as appropriate.

Refer to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary any unsolved disagreements or issues concerning the substance or potential policy implications of the proposed NPI or to the priority accorded to the proposed NPI’s review that emerged during the clearance process.
Ensure that systems for receiving, reviewing, tracking, concurring are in place.

Ensure that systems for receiving, reviewing, tracking, concurring, and issuing NPIs are effective for consistently complying with requirements of this instruction (e.g., electronic tracking, paperless sign-off grids, and e-folders).

Consider each proposed NPI (and any associated critical policy or non-policy issues) and promptly respond to the originator regarding technical considerations, potential program/policy impacts, Agency-wide implications, and anticipated reception.
Initiate and develop proposed issuance of the new or revised policy and procedures, emphasizing the need for policy to be communicated through the CFR, the FR, or the OSHA Directives System.
Ensure reviewers receive the appropriate background information to be considered in the appropriate context.
Obtain guidance, as appropriate, and clearance from the Office of the Solicitor.

Obtain input, technical or otherwise, and policy clearance of proposed issuances/directives from each program director, free-standing office, regional administrator, and others identified as mandatory clearance offices.
Refer to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary for resolution.
Consider each proposed issuance (and any associated critical policy or non-policy issues).
Consider each proposed issuance (and any associated critical policy issues) and promptly respond to the originator.
Develop and publish written procedures that define and specify mandatory clearance processes and requirements.
Consult with the Office of the Solicitor or Regional Office of the Solicitor.
Perform notification and rapid review screening for Directions.
Transmit all directives originating in the National Office to all Program Directors.
Clear the transmitted directives by all Program Directors.
Consult with the Director of the Directorate of Cooperative and [State Programs] (Director of DEP).
Communicate frequently with Agency offices and stakeholders to agree on policies, procedures, and required actions during the initial drafting process.
Informally circulate drafts, reports, and other materials relating to a directive drafting before conducting the formal clearance process.
Consult with DEP and DCSP for federal program change language during the informal clearance.
Ensure construction-related directives are reviewed by DOC.
Elevate disputes with clearance officials to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary for resolution.
Notify affected parties when the approved directive is posted on the directives webpage as necessary.
Review the draft directive. If necessary, request an extension from the originator as soon as possible.
Complete item number 9 and 10 of the OSHA 201.
Return the OSHA 201 with comments to the originator. Alternatively, sends concurrence or comments electronically (e.g., email, shared folders, SharePoint, etc.).
Negotiate the acceptance of comments with the originator or originating Program Director or presents the case to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary for resolution.
Review the directive clearance packet to determine if complete and accurate for approval clearance.
Provide revisions request from the Office of the Assistant Secretary (OAS) to the originator for necessary updates.
Provide notification of signed directive to the originator and requests the directive be formatted to a 508-compliant PDF file.
Coordinate union review through the Labor Management Relations Officer.
Assign an effective date based on the approval by the union, delayed posting request, or pending press release.
Provide the final 508-compliant PDF file directive to the web administrator for posting to the Directives webpage.
Review 508-compliant PDF file for web-related technical content.
Publish 508-compliant PDF file to OSHA Directives webpage within the requested timeframe.
Notify the directives officer of availability on the OSHA Directives webpage.
Confirm the current directive template is being used.
Tentatively identify the type of directive and assign a subject following consultation with the directives officer.
Provide each directive with a classification code and numeric number based on the type of directive.
Assign consecutive numbers (Directives Officer for national directives, or Regional Administrators for regional directives).
Number OSHA Instructions according to primary, secondary, and tertiary classification codes and sequentially by primary classification.
Number OSHA Notices using the calendar year and a two-digit sequential number followed by the subject's primary and secondary codes (or cancellation format if applicable).
Number OSHA Directions the same as Notices, preceding with the DIR identifier.
Conduct review of non-substantive changes by the Program Director (or Regional Administrator), the directives officer, and the Office of the Solicitor.

Conduct review and obtain approval for non-significant changes from the Program Director (or Regional Administrator), expert directorates, Directorate of Administrative Programs, directives officer, Assistant Secretary's Office, and the Office of the Solicitor.
Submit significant changes to undergo the normal clearance process.
Implement and comply with the provisions of this instruction and with related instructions governing the management of policy and non-policy issues.
Self-assess and routinely monitor the directive process using the checklist on the directives tools webpage to ensure compliance with the provisions of the instruction.
Review and assess active directives on an annual basis with a final document of the review findings maintained in the national office or regional files.
Conduct a full assessment of the files for the directives approved that year and ensure all appropriate documents are included in the file.

Send an email or memo to the Director, Office of Audit, DAP, or designee documenting the completion of the requirement no later than September 30 of the fiscal year, and provide documentation to the directives officer.
Retain completion documentation in the directive file record.
Confirm the self-assessment documentation was received within the established deadline in the first quarter of each fiscal year.
Include the auditing of the assessment process as part of audit responsibilities and ensure that the directives process is part of regular auditing tasks.

preconditions.text: There are unsolved disagreements or issues concerning the substance or potential policy implications of the proposed policy issuance or the priority according to its review that emerged during the clearance process.
Program Director and Regional Administrator receiving a policy issuance for review and clearance
There is doubt regarding whether the Office of the Solicitor should review and clear an NPI.
The directive is a Direction (time-sensitive policy and procedure pronouncement).
The directive originates in the National Office.
The approved directive is posted on the directives webpage.
The proposed changes are non-substantive (correcting typographical errors, grammar, syntax, minor factual inaccuracies, or updating outdated references).
The proposed changes are non-significant (do not significantly alter the requirements of the directive).
The proposed changes are significant (major substantive changes that change the purpose or intent, or substantially alter OSHA policies and procedures).

constraints.text: Allow no more than three weeks turnaround time for response return.

Allow no less than two weeks turnaround time. OSHA Directions which are time-sensitive may have a shorter turnaround time. Additional clearance reviews, except Directions, require a minimum of five working days.
NPIs do not establish or amend Agency policy or procedures, regardless of their format or medium.
Non-policy guidance materials must be reliable, accurate, easy to understand, locate, and reference.
Written procedures must specify formatting requirements, clearance timeframes, comment resolution processes, mandatory review/clearance officials, and background information sharing methods.

Written procedures must ensure views of subordinate staff, clearance officials, and the Office of the Solicitor are considered. The Office of the Solicitor must clear NPIs containing legal constructions, citations, or opinions.
Written procedures must specify officials authorized for NPI release approval, recordkeeping (retained per OSHA policies), and procedure maintenance.
Directions remain in effect until the superseding instruction is effective but not more than 12 months from the effective date or until canceled by a superseding directive, whichever occurs first.
Notices must not remain in effect over one year, except for permanent cancellations of existing instructions or Notices.

Regional directives must provide direction on subjects not covered by a national directive, must not alter or contradict any statement in a national directive, and must be consistent with the format outlined in national directives.
Regional Administrators must follow guidance in this instruction when assigning consecutive numbers.
For extensive web-page directives (FOM, SHMS, OTM), only the specific chapter being updated must be sent through the clearance process.
Program Directors and Regional Administrators may delegate the authority of review and assessment, as well as responsibility for preparing a final document of the review findings, as appropriate.
Must be completed no later than September 30 of the fiscal year.
